title: Decision on how the European Commission followed-up on a judgment of the Court of Justice of the EU that Spain infringed EU law (case 2183/2024/(OAM)PGP)
text:
Background to the complaint 1. On 14 June 2017, the European Commission opened a formal infringement procedure against Spain by issuing a letter of formal notice [1] on the grounds that its national rules on State liability for a breach of EU law due to the Spanish legislature were contrary to the principles of equivalence and/or effectiveness.

2. On 25 January 2018, the Commission sent a reasoned opinion to Spain. [2] 3. On 27 November 2019, the Commission decided to refer Spain to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) regarding Spanish rules on compensation for damages caused by the State when adopting legislation contrary to Union law. [3] 4. On 28 June 2022, the CJEU adopted a judgment in case C-278/20 (‘the judgment’) in which it held that certain provisions, contained in two Spanish laws [4] and regulating the compensation for the loss or harm caused to individuals by the Spanish legislature as a result of an infringement of EU law, were themselves in breach of the principle of effectiveness.

5. In November 2024, the complainant turned to the Ombudsman [5] complaining about the time taken by the Commission to ensure Spain’s compliance with the judgment and its delay in referring the matter to the CJEU for a second time under Article 260(2) TFEU [6] . The inquiry 6. The Ombudsman opened an inquiry into the time taken by the Commission to ensure Spain’s compliance with the judgment.

7. In the course of the inquiry, the Ombudsman received the reply of the Commission to the complaint and, subsequently, the comments of the complainant in response to the Commission’s reply. The Ombudsman inquiry team also inspected electronically some documents contained in the Commission’s file on this case. Subsequently, the Ombudsman inquiry team met with representatives of the Commission and inspected additional documents during the meeting. The Ombudsman invited the complainant to provide comments on the inspection report concerning the documents inspected electronically and on the meeting report and the complainant did so. Arguments presented to the Ombudsman By the Commission 8. The following timeline on the Commission’s handling of the matter was presented during the inquiry: On 27 October 2022, the Commission sent an administrative letter to Spain. Between 28 November 2022 and 2 May 2023, Spain sent three letters to the Commission. On 23 July 2023, general elections took place in Spain. On 15 November 2023, a new Prime Minister was inaugurated in Spain. Between 29 July 2024 and 2 October 2024, the Commission and Spain held two meetings. On 7 January 2025, Spain sent draft legislative amendments to the Commission aimed at complying with the judgment. On 10 February 2025, the Commission sent an e-mail to Spain. On 14 March 2025, Spain sent new draft legislative amendments to the Commission. On 18 March 2025, Spain and the Commission held a meeting. On 26 March 2025 and then again on 7 April 2025, Spain sent new draft legislative amendments to the Commission On 23 April 2025, Spain and the Commission held a meeting. On 24 April 2025, Spain sent new draft legislative amendments to the Commission. On 30 April 2025, the Spanish Council of Ministers sent its preliminary draft law to the Council of State. On 7 October 2025, the Spanish Council of Ministers adopted the draft law and sent it to the Spanish Congress of Deputies. On 8 October 2025, the Commission requested Spain to provide explanatory information. On 30 October 2025, Spain sent some information to the Commission. On 6 November 2025, Spain sent further information to the Commission. On 10 November 2025, the Commission sent an e-mail to Spain. On 11 December 2025, the Commission sent, pursuant to Article 260(2) TFEU, a letter of formal notice to Spain. On 17 December 2025, the Commission sent a letter to the complainant.

9. The Commission explained that, since the delivery of the judgment, it had been in contact with the Spanish authorities. Several exchanges took place during the period 27 October 2022 to 2 May 2023. Internal discussions took place within the Commission between July 2023 and July 2024 and some requests were sent to Spain during this period. Between July 2024 and March 2025, three meetings were held between representatives of the Commission and representatives of Spain regarding the measures taken by Spain to comply with the judgment. Spain sent draft legislative amendments to the Commission on four occasions during the period January to April 2025.

10. The Commission considered the preliminary draft law that Spain shared with it on 24 April 2025 to implement the judgment. However, on 7 October 2025, the draft law, adopted by the Spanish Council of Ministers and sent to the Spanish Congress of Deputies, included some changes compared to the preliminary draft law of April 2025. Considering that the new changes contributed to the delay in ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment, the Commission sent Spain a letter of formal notice in December 2025.

11. Regarding the time taken, the Commission explained that, according to its internal guidelines, its services must investigate cases concerning Member States’ failure to comply with CJEU judgments (Article 260 TFEU cases) with a view to arriving at a decision to refer the matter to the CJEU for a second time or to close the case within 8 to 18 months. However, the time taken to come to a conclusion depends on each specific case. If there is a willingness from the Member State concerned to advance in the legislative changes needed to comply with the CJEU judgment, it is more efficient to continue the dialogue with the Member State rather than to immediately send a letter of formal notice under Article 260(2) TFEU. In this case, the delay in ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment was also affected by the re-assignment of the infringement procedure between different Commission departments and the general elections that took place in Spain in July 2023, which halted the measures taken by Spain to comply with the judgment until there was a new government. By the complainant 12. The complainant stated that Spanish citizens are in a situation of legal uncertainty when it comes to making claims for compensation due to loss or harm caused to individuals by the Spanish legislature as a result of an infringement of EU law. More than three years since the delivery of the judgment, Spain has not yet brought its legislation in line with EU law to comply with the judgment. According to EU case-law [7] , a Member State cannot plead provisions, practices or situations prevailing in its domestic legal order to justify failure to observe obligations arising under EU law.

13. According to EU case-law, the process of compliance by the Member State concerned with a CJEU judgment must be initiated at once and must be completed as soon as possible. [8] In the complainant’s opinion, the Commission does not have discretion in ensuring compliance by the Member State concerned with the CJEU judgment.

14. The Commission has failed to comply with its internal guidelines that foresee that cases where Member States have failed to comply with a CJEU judgment must be pursued as a matter of priority and that the Commission must investigate those cases with a view to arriving at a decision to refer the matter to the CJEU for a second time or to close the case within 8 to 18 months. The Commission must comply with its internal guidelines to ensure administrative coherence, transparency and to avoid the perception of arbitrariness. The Commission’s internal guidelines create a legitimate expectation that they will be applied and they impose limits on the Commission’s discretion.

15. In the case at stake, the Spanish government is using tactics to delay Spain’s compliance with the judgment. The delay is also due to the Commission’s failure to comply with its responsibilities.

16. According to the complainant, all the documentation that the Spanish Council of Ministers sent to the Spanish Congress of Deputies in October 2025 dates back to 2023, which shows that the file has been dormant for a couple of years. Moreover, the complainant further argued that the Spanish Congress of Deputies has been postponing multiple times the deadline for submitting amendments to the draft law of October 2025. The Ombudsman's assessment 17. The role of the Ombudsman in this case is to determine whether the time taken by the Commission to ensure Spain’s compliance with the judgment is justified and compatible with the principles of good administration.

18. The Commission’s handling of this matter relates to Spain’s compliance with a CJEU judgment. Even though the EU Treaties do not specify the period within which a CJEU judgment must be complied with, it follows from EU case-law that the importance of immediate and uniform application of EU law requires that the process of compliance by the Member State concerned must be initiated at once and must be completed as soon as possible. [9] The CJEU delivered its judgment on 28 June 2022 and Spain has not yet brought its legislation in line with EU law to comply with the judgment.

19. According to Article 260(2) TFEU, when the Member State concerned has not taken the necessary measures to comply with the CJEU judgment, the Commission may bring the case before the CJEU after giving that Member State the opportunity to submit its observations. While the Commission therefore has discretion in deciding whether and when to make use of the power conferred to it by Article 260(2) TFEU, the Ombudsman considers that it should, as a matter of good administration, be diligent and active in monitoring the compliance of the Member State concerned with the judgment. The Commission should do so in accordance with its internal guidelines, within a reasonable time and without undue delays.

20. The Ombudsman’s view on this matter is supported by the Commission’s internal guidelines of 18 July 2017 “Monitoring the application of EU law”, which set out that the Commission will normally pursue, as a matter of priority, cases where Member States have failed to comply with a CJEU judgment. The same internal guidelines foresee that the Commission services must investigate those cases with a view to arriving at a decision to refer the matter to the CJEU for a second time or to close the case within 8 to 18 months.

21. The Commission first contacted Spain to enquire about the implementation of the judgment on 27 October 2022, that is four months after the judgment. Between November 2022 and May 2023, Spain sent replies to the Commission. Given that implementing the judgment appeared to require legislative amendments, which takes time, these initial steps appear reasonable.

22. Between May 2023 and July 2024, the documents inspected in the Commission’s file do not indicate any action by the Commission. Although the Commission argues that internal discussions took place within the Commission during this period and that some requests were sent to Spain, no documentation to substantiate this exists in the Commission’s file. However, based on the information gathered during the inquiry, it appears that the delay in progressing on the matter during this period can be partly attributed to the situation in Spain. The general elections, which took place in Spain in July 2023, halted the measures taken by Spain to comply with the judgment until there was a new government in November 2023.

23. Between July 2024 and April 2025, four meetings were held between representatives of Spain and representatives of the Commission. In addition, between January 2025 and April 2025, Spain sent draft legislative amendments to the Commission on four occasions. This shows that, as from July 2024, the Commission continued to be active on the file, and that the matter evolved at national level.

24. The Commission considered the preliminary draft law that Spain shared with it on 24 April 2025 to implement the judgment. However, on 7 October 2025, the draft law, adopted by the Spanish Council of Ministers and sent to the Spanish Congress of Deputies, included some changes compared to the preliminary draft law of April 2025. Those new changes contributed to the delay in ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment. Based on the above, the complainant’s argument that no substantive changes were made to the Spanish documentation since 2023 does not appear substantiated.

25. Based on the inquiry, the Ombudsman finds that, as of July 2024, the Commission actively exchanged with Spain on the matter up to 11 December 2025, when the Commission sent another letter of formal notice to Spain. This shows the Commission’s continued efforts to take action aimed at ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment.

26. In view of the above, and despite the period of one year without any proof of action by the Commission, the Ombudsman considers that the Commission has overall acted reasonably in following the matter, given the circumstances of this case and in particular the time needed by Spain to complete the legislative process.

27. Although the Commission’s internal guidelines are an important tool for ensuring progress, the Ombudsman considers reasonable the Commission’s explanations that, when a Member State shows willingness to take action, a dialogue may be the most efficient way forward. In this case, the timeline and explanations of the steps taken do not lead to a finding that the Commission manifestly acted outside its discretion in this regard.

28. As a result, although the matter has not yet reached a final conclusion, the Ombudsman considers that no further inquiries are justified at this stage.

29. The Ombudsman trusts that, following the sending of the letter of formal notice to Spain pursuant to Article 260(2) TFEU, the Commission will keep monitoring Spain’s compliance with the judgment actively, diligently and without further delay. Conclusion Based on the inquiry, the Ombudsman closes this case with the following conclusion: Given the steps taken by the Commission so far, including the recent letter of formal notice, and considering the circumstances of the case that explain the timeline of the Commission’s actions, no further inquiries are justified at this stage . The Ombudsman trusts that the Commission will keep monitoring the matter actively, diligently and without further delay. The complainant and the Commission will be informed of this decision . Teresa Anjinho European Ombudsman Strasbourg, 10/03/2026 [1] https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/memo_17_1577 [2] https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/memo_18_349 [3] https://ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/ip_19_6299 [4] Ley 40/2015 de Régimen Jurídico del Sector Público (Law 40/2015 on the legal system governing the public sector) and Ley 39/2015 del Procedimiento Administrativo Común de las Administraciones Públicas (Law 39/2015 on the common administrative procedure of the public authorities). [5] The complainant had already turned to the Ombudsman in April 2023 and February 2024 about how the Commission was ensuring Spain’s compliance with the judgment. At that time, the Ombudsman found insufficient grounds to inquire into the matter. [6] According to Article 260(2) TFEU, if the Commission considers that the Member State concerned has not taken the necessary measures to comply with the judgment of the Court, it may bring the case before the Court after giving that State the opportunity to submit its observations. [7] See, for instance, of the Court of Justice of the European Union of 25 February 2021, European Commission V Kingdom of Spain , C-658/19, paragraph 19: https://infocuria.curia.europa.eu/tabs/document/C/2019/C-0658-19-00000000RD-01-P-01/ARRET/238164-EN-1-html [8] See, for instance, judgment of the Court of Justice of the European Union, Commission of the European Communities v French Republic , C-334/94 , paragraph 31: https://infocuria.curia.europa.eu/tabs/document/C/1994/C-0334-94-00000000RD-01-P-01/ARRET/100064-EN-1-pdf [9] See, for instance, judgment of the Court of Justice of the European Union, Commission of the European Communities v French Republic , C-334/94 , paragraph 31: https://infocuria.curia.europa.eu/tabs/document/C/1994/C-0334-94-00000000RD-01-P-01/ARRET/100064-EN-1-pdf
summary: The case concerned the time taken by the European Commission to ensure Spain’s compliance with a judgment of the Court of Justice of the European Union concerning Spain’s national rules as regards State liability for breaches of EU law. The complainant was concerned that the Commission was not taking timely and effective actions to ensure Spain’s compliance with the judgment. The Ombudsman found that the Commission had generally followed the matter actively from the adoption of the judgment. Although there was a period of approximately one year showing no documented trace of any action taken by the Commission, the time taken to pursue the matter could be partly attributed to the situation in Spain. In addition, the Ombudsman found reasonable the Commission’s position that, when a Member State shows willingness to take action, a dialogue may be the most efficient way forward. The Ombudsman therefore concluded that, given the steps taken by the Commission so far, including the recent letter of formal notice sent to Spain, and considering the circumstances of the case that explain the timeline of the Commission’s actions, no further inquiries were justified at that point in time and closed the case.
